name: Jason Yates
subject: A Special Prayer for Your Children
body:
| | Happy Father’s Day
---
[\- ](https://www2.myfaithvotes.org/r?u=6_nuz-gPbh4AR2HufX66icYcIyRhB2URpXghNXmPGvMYMstL8oA_8CyJDNT_svW-RJL2ICPJllQNM3OLkMvjvw&e=be53806c85fc17bd685e1e4c4d896e92&utm_source=myfaithvotes&utm_medium=email&utm_campaign=e190616com01&n=1)
| Peter,

Pastor Todd Wagner of Watermark Church in Dallas, Texas, has [written a special prayer](https://www2.myfaithvotes.org/r?u=6_nuz-gPbh4AR2HufX66icYcIyRhB2URpXghNXmPGvMYMstL8oA_8CyJDNT_svW-RJL2ICPJllQNM3OLkMvjvw&e=be53806c85fc17bd685e1e4c4d896e92&utm_source=myfaithvotes&utm_medium=email&utm_campaign=e190616com01&n=2), included below, that will encourage you this Father's Day. In our culture, the role of a father in a child's life has been diminished and devalued. And if we as Christian fathers hope to buck the trend and live up to the calling God has asked of us in fatherhood, then we must pray faithfully and fervently for our children. Here is a prayer I've written for one of my sons.

My prayer for you, my vision for your life, is that as a result of this gift of God’s Word:

One day you will be able to answer the most complex problems in the world with confidence.

One day you will be able to face the darkest and most difficult of circumstances with hope.

One day you will be sought out by multitudes to provide them comfort, guidance and leadership in their distress and you will be found ready, full of unfailing confidence that you can serve them well.

One day you will be able to stand firm against the passions of your flesh, unwaveringly remaining pure though all around you fall to the irresistible titillations of the moment.

One day you will be able to speak with profound insight, great courage and unwavering boldness while others are uncertain, constantly changing and consumed with the opinions of others.

One day you will be a pillar of strength for a world ready to collapse under the weight of its foolishness.

One day you will be able to stand strong when others crumble.

One day you will speak and others will be fed by the fruit, which pours forth from your lips.

One day you will be able to reveal to others what no eye has seen, no ear has heard and you will be able to unveil powerfully truths that have never entered into the hearts of man.

One day you will be able to restore souls, rejoice hearts, enlighten eyes and inform minds with perfection.

One day you will be ready to step up and lead courageously, speak out boldly, stand firm bravely, stay humble continually and serve the One true King nobly.

One day you will be blessed because you will delight yourself in the law of the Lord, and on that law you will meditate day and night. No man is wise who is ignorant of God’s word and no man is uneducated who knows the Bible’s teachings

The man who doesn’t read the Bible has no advantage of the man who has no Bible. Apply your whole self to the text...apply the whole text to yourself.

Read it to be wise, believe it to be safe, practice it to be holy, share it to be useful. Read it through, pray it in, live it out, pass it on.

[](https://www2.myfaithvotes.org/r?u=6_nuz-gPbh4AR2HufX66icYcIyRhB2URpXghNXmPGvMYMstL8oA_8CyJDNT_svW-RJL2ICPJllQNM3OLkMvjvw&e=be53806c85fc17bd685e1e4c4d896e92&utm_source=myfaithvotes&utm_medium=email&utm_campaign=e190616com01&n=3) **United. We Stand.**

Jason Yates
CEO
[](https://www2.myfaithvotes.org/r?u=FvZODUdzRCV4_QVEoUI5Y_LYDGbWPUdf00keax2EMv8&e=be53806c85fc17bd685e1e4c4d896e92&utm_source=myfaithvotes&utm_medium=email&utm_campaign=e190616com01&n=4)

name: Andrew Gillum
subject: what father's day means to me
disclaimer_text: Paid for by Forward Florida Action,
body:
[](http://act.andrewgillum.com/page/m/fcb359f/9e00690/6a7924c0/2407158c/736460080/VEsH/ "https://secure.actblue.com/donate/fwd-fla-action?refcode=em-header&utm_medium=email&utm_source=gillum&utm_content=1&utm_campaign=061619-es-fathers-day-nfr&source=061619-es-fathers-day-nfr")

Friends,

Father's Day will always remind me of my dad, Charles "Sonny" Gillum. Not because he was perfect, but because he was the best of men, who got up every day to work in the hot Florida sun to provide for our family.

My father taught me the value of hard work, and for that I am forever grateful.

Although this is the first Father's Day without my dad here with us, I'm comforted that in his life he never met a stranger, and I pray that he's among friends and at peace, until we meet again.

The future my father wanted for me, and the dreams I have for my children will forever guide me as I fight for the future of children across our nation.

As a father to three beautiful children myself, it's my duty to do everything I can to ensure they grow up in a world filled with hope and the opportunity to accomplish anything they set their mind to.

I'll never stop working until this is our reality.

Together, we can give our children the future they deserve.

It's not going to be easy, but like my dad would always say, you've got to put in the work to reap the reward. And I hope you'll join me to put in the work to help build a future that's as bright as it can be.

To all the fellow dads out there and families celebrating today, from the bottom of my heart, I wish you a Happy Father's Day.

Andrew Gillum

name: Michael Bennet
subject: This Father's Day
disclaimer_text: Paid for by Bennet for America
body:
| | | This Father's Day, I'm reminded how our kids shape our future.

Raising three intelligent and compassionate young women has taught Susan and me so much more than we could have ever imagined. They've shaped every aspect of our lives, including my decision to run for President.

[](https://secure.michaelbennet.com/page/m/35cba264/134673f2/251e346/6996c2e2/2786009047/VEsH/ "https://store.michaelbennet.com/?refcode=EM_FR_B1_061619_AC_ND&recurring=auto&amount=1&amounts=3,5,10,25,100,250")
As a parent, you want to give your children the world. You make sure they are safe, healthy, and empowered to live a life they love. And you do your best to leave them with more than you had yourself.

That's why I'm determined to combat climate change and leave this planet healthier than we inherited it.

That's why I'm committed to protecting health care for all Americans.

That's why I'm focused on building a 21st-century education system that lifts people up, instead of reinforcing the economic inequality we see today.

I'm running for president with this approach to every issue: _Will it help or hurt the next generation?_

[](https://secure.michaelbennet.com/page/m/35cba264/134673f2/251e346/6996c2e2/2786009047/VEsE/ "https://store.michaelbennet.com/?refcode=EM_FR_B1_061619_AC_ND&recurring=auto&amount=1&amounts=3,5,10,25,100,250")
For the future of my daughters and kids all across the country, I'm ready to build a better world.

Michael

name: Elizabeth Warren
subject: I wouldn't be here if my daddy hadn't pushed open a door so I could have a chance
disclaimer_text: Paid for by Warren for President
body:
Happy Father's Day to all the daddies who are trying their hardest to give their children a strong future -- and who set the example every day of what it means to care for the people you love. | | | [](https://my.elizabethwarren.com/page/m/2852e05c/45c30650/16c016de2/669b654d/2118403168/VEsH/ "https://elizabethwarren.com")
---
I wish I could call my daddy today.

After my mother died, I called him every night. Most weekends, we watched sports together, with Daddy in Oklahoma and me in Massachusetts, and we called back and forth on the phone after a really good (or bad) play.

I wanted to keep him company, and I wanted to hear his voice.

In the 1920s, my daddy had big dreams. He wanted to fly airplanes. He was barely out of high school when he rebuilt a two-passenger, open-cockpit airplane and taught himself to fly above the prairies of eastern Oklahoma. I always pictured him landing and taking off in vast wheat fields, a tiny plane in an immense blue sky.

He survived the double blows of the Dust Bowl and the Great Depression in the small town where he grew up. Decades later, both my parents still talked of bank failures and families who lost their farms during some of the toughest times for our country.

By the time World War II came along, my mother and daddy already had three young boys. Daddy tried to enlist to be a fighter pilot in the war, but the Army Air Forces (as it was known then) said he was too old, or at least that's the explanation I heard.

When the war finally ended, Daddy desperately wanted a job flying the new passenger planes for one of the fast-growing airlines like TWA or American. But that didn't work out either. My mother told me that those jobs also went to younger men.

So Daddy moved from one job to another: He was a salesman, he fixed cars, he was a janitor. He liked working with his hands, doing repairs around the house. And like a zillion other families, we got by.

When I was a senior in high school, I started thinking about college. I wanted to be a teacher, and that meant I needed a college diploma. But my mother said it was out of the question. She pointed out that we couldn't afford college, that she and Daddy just didn't make enough money. Besides, my daddy had had a heart attack, and now it took both of my parents' paychecks to manage.

I knew how much my parents sacrificed for my future and for my brothers' future, but I wanted to teach. It was my dream, and I wouldn't give up.

My mother kept saying no, and we argued back and forth. Then Daddy surprised both of us, saying: "Let her try, Polly." And I was off and running.

There were lots of bumps and wrong turns along the way. I got married at 19 (it didn't work out). I moved. I dropped out of school. But eventually, I made it.

Whatever I did, my daddy believed in me. He would say, "That's my Betsy."

In truth, I think he found it pretty miraculous that his baby girl had ended up a teacher. He nearly busted his buttons the first time I was quoted in the newspaper.

When my daddy got sick, I came across a video series about the airplanes of World War II. I bought all the videotapes and took them to him. I was sure he would love seeing the old planes, that he would point out this or that plane he remembered from the war. But the only thing he wanted to talk about was how much he missed my mother.

For months -- for years, actually -- after he died, I would see or hear something and think, _Oh, I 'll tell Daddy about that._ And then I'd get a little jolt all over again.

I would not be here had it not been for my daddy pushing open a door so that I could have a chance. That is how this daughter of a janitor ended up as a public school teacher, a college professor, a U.S. senator, and a candidate for president of the United States. And I'm not the only one. **Parents all across our country are working hard to give their children the opportunities they themselves never had.

That kind of sacrifice -- a sacrifice fueled by love -- is why I'm in this fight all the way.** All of our children deserve a chance to succeed. And all of our parents deserve the peace of mind knowing their hard work created a better future for their children and grandchildren. That is the promise of America, and I'm fighting to make sure we keep that promise.

My daddy is gone, but I want to wish him a Happy Father's Day. And Happy Father's Day to all the daddies who are trying their hardest to give their children a strong future -- and who set the example every day of what it means to care for the people you love.

Happy Father's Day!

Elizabeth

He's a great dad who has worked hard as attorney general to protect other Mississippi children and families. He's prosecuted those who prey on children on the internet, he's fought cyberbullying, and he's worked with law enforcement to combat human trafficking.

Today, we're celebrating our Dad and all that he does. Thanks for supporting his campaign!

Rebecca, Matthew, and Annabelle Hood

name: Teresa Tomlinson
subject: My role model, Bob Pike
disclaimer_text: PAID FOR BY TERESA TOMLINSON FOR SENATE
body:
Friend –
https://secure.actblue.com/donate/tt-fathers-day?refcode=em061619&noskip=true [https://secure.actblue.com/donate/tt-fathers-day?refcode=em061619&noskip=true] My father, Bob Pike, was born in a farming community in Coggon, Iowa, the only child of a 53-year-old farmer and a 41-year-old school teacher. By the time he was a teenager, both of his parents suffered from disabilities. My grandmother had a heart condition that eventually took her life, and my grandfather struggled with (what was then) life-altering effects of a broken hip. With both of them unable to work, they lived a meager, humble life.
Dad joined the Air Force and met my Mom at a soda fountain in Moultrie while serving at Moody Air Force Base in Valdosta, Georgia. They were both young and poor, but together they built a life for themselves based on hard work and their love for each other — she was, and is, the love of his life.
My father supported his parents throughout his military service and while pursuing his degree – because that's what good sons do. When my grandmother passed, Dad petitioned to have his father moved into University of Georgia student housing as a dependent to him and my Mom. It worked, even though the Dean said no student had ever asked for such a thing before.
But that was my Dad: Family first, always.
I promise to work every day for the people of our state and to be a model of character and integrity, just like my father brought his girls up to be. But it’s going to take all of us working together to win this race. If you are able, please chip in now to support my campaign for U.S. Senate. [https://secure.actblue.com/donate/tt-fathers-day?refcode=em061619&noskip=true]
DONATE [https://secure.actblue.com/donate/tt-fathers-day?refcode=em061619&noskip=true]
As a father, my Dad set an extraordinary example of character and commitment that my sister and I strive to meet every day. My Dad coached our softball teams, sat (seemingly) interested during ballet recitals, and always cheered the loudest — because that’s what good fathers do.
Though he is a quiet, reserved man — his patience, compassion, and love are immense. On this day, we celebrate the Dads of the world.
My Dad is the sweetest man I know.
Happy Father’s Day to my sweet Dad and all the Dads out there.

name: Joe Biden
subject: What my dad taught me:
disclaimer_text: Paid for by Biden for President
body:
| [ ](https://go.joebiden.com/page/m/2931949f/1cd0ac28/2d086618/356ce7f8/112336289/VEsH/ "https://www.joebiden.com")
---
My dad, Joseph Robinette Biden Sr., was a good man. What I learned from him, I learned from watching.

He'd been knocked down as a young man, but he never stopped trying. He was the first one up in our house every morning, clean-shaven, well dressed, putting on the coffee, getting ready to go to work.

My dad taught me the value of effort and work, and he taught me about shouldering burdens with grace.

He didn't judge a man by how many times he got knocked down, but by how fast he got up.

Get up. That was his phrase, and it's echoed throughout my life. Bad day? Get up. The world dropped you on your head? Get up. Lying in bed feeling sorry for yourself? Get up.

Long after he passed away, my dad's wisdom has continued to get me through some of the hardest moments of my life. And I'll never stop being grateful.

My wish for all of you, today and everyday, is that you have someone in your life who steadies you and gives you the resolve to move forward -- to keep getting up -- the way my dad did for me.

Happy Father's Day.

\- Joe

body:

| | Peter,

Most Kentuckians know that Andy is a fighter for hard working families. But the Andy that I know best is, first and foremost, a great dad to our two kids.

Between our family, our church, and the occasional Little League games he still gets to coach, Andy is pretty busy these days serving as attorney general and running for governor.

So today I'm taking a moment to wish him a happy Father's Day and to thank him for being a great dad to our kids.

While he's been in office, he's defended Kentuckians who have seen their lives turned upside down by opioid companies. He's stood alongside teachers facing attack after attack from Frankfort politicians. And he's fought to get survivors of abuse and assault justice and closure.

Andy knows that it's not about picking political fights or helping special interests -- it's about making sure that we lift up every community across the commonwealth, and give all Kentuckians access to stronger schools, better jobs, and affordable health care no matter where they live.

name: John Hickenlooper
subject: The man I called "Hick"
body:
Hick, who had lost his own father young, loved being a father--maybe more than anything. | | | [](https://secure.hickenlooper.com/page/m/637717d2/691c9bd/6dfd678f/11a4c91c/3487023885/VEsH/ "https://secure.actblue.com/donate/hickenlooper2020-130kdonors?refcode=em_template")
---
| I miss him every day, friend. [](https://secure.hickenlooper. "https://secure.actblue.com/donate/hickenlooper2020-130kdonors?refcode=em_fr_20190616_JH_20190616-fr-JH-fathersday-all_all_i1&recurring=auto&email=dpwillis67@gmail.com&firstname=&lastname=&city=Englewood&state=FL&zip=34223&phone=&occupation=&employer=") When my father (full name: John Hickenlooper Sr.) married my mother, she was a widow with two children. Rather than claim the name "Dad," he invited them to call him by his nickname, "Hick." Then my sister Deborah and I came along--his two biological children--but he was Hick to us, too. He wanted us to feel like one family. And we did.

**Hick, who had lost his own father young, loved being a father --maybe more than anything.**

He called us his "tadpoles" and his "jewels." He loved introducing us to things he loved, like summer storms, fire engines, and _The Wizard of Oz_. He loved piling all of us in the car and just taking off, letting us navigate for him, embracing the adventure of getting lost together.

An engineer by trade, he loved taking things apart--often on the living room rug, to my mom's consternation--and putting them back together. He loved challenges, especially mental ones, and loved asking us to rise to them.

He fostered curiosity and boldness in his children, taking one child out for dinner when she opened her eyes underwater, celebrating another for beating him in chess. He took endless joy in watching us grow. [](https://secure.hickenlooper. "https://secure.actblue.com/donate/hickenlooper2020-130kdonors?refcode=em_fr_20190616_JH_20190616-fr-JH-fathersday-all_all_i2&recurring=auto&email=dpwillis67@gmail.com&firstname=&lastname=&city=Englewood&state=FL&zip=34223&phone=&occupation=&employer=")
_Hick with the whole family (I 'm the baby he's holding)._ He loved fatherhood, family, and life so much that what life had in store for him--and us--was unusually cruel. By the time I was seven years old, cancer had hollowed out his body. Ten days after my eighth birthday, he died.

I've lived with Hick's ghost my whole life: as a young man fumbling his way to adulthood, as a grown man transitioning careers and learning to navigate relationships, and--for the last seventeen years--as a father myself. And there are times along the way when I'm sure having Hick by my side, a phone call or (nowadays) a FaceTime away, would have helped me do things better.

But as my Mom told me after Hick passed: **" You can't control what life throws at you, but you can control how you respond."**

Even in his last days of illness, Hick showed so much strength and patience. Decades later, when as a Governor I guided our state through challenges horrifying and humbling--wildfires, flooding, shootings--I thought of my father, and rallied.

**Father 's Day means many things to many people.** For some, it celebrates a relationship that continues to nourish and sustain. For others, it may be a day of pain or anger, perhaps at a father figure who has disappointed. For me, it's a mix: yearning for the years unspent with Hick, even as I eagerly watch my own son grow into the man he's meant to be.

I don't think it's an understatement to say our country needs a new model of masculinity--one that is open, secure, unafraid to be emotional, to nurture, to love. The opposite surrounds us these days, everywhere from news headlines to the White House itself.

But by keeping alive the spirit of Hick--and men like him who fearlessly commit to love, support and empower others--I think we can turn this around.

**I think we can do it.** [](https://secure.hickenlooper. "https://secure.actblue.com/donate/hickenlooper2020-130kdonors?refcode=em_fr_20190616_JH_20190616-fr-JH-fathersday-all_all_i3&email=dpwillis67@gmail.com&firstname=&lastname=&city=Englewood&state=FL&zip=34223&phone=&occupation=&employer=") With love,
John **[](https://secure.hickenlooper. "https://secure.actblue.com/donate/hickenlooper2020-130kdonors?refcode=em_fr_20190616_JH_20190616-fr-JH-fathersday-all_all_contribbutton&email=dpwillis67@gmail.com&firstname=&lastname=&city=Englewood&state=FL&zip=34223&phone=&occupation=&employer=")**

name: Bliss and Jaymie Collins
subject: Our dad, Wilmot Collins
disclaimer_text: Paid for by A whole lot of folks for Wilmot Collins, PO Box 458, Helena, MT 59624
body:
#
Team,
This Father's Day, we want to take a moment to tell you a little bit about our dad, Wilmot Collins.
Dad's journey began in Liberia, where he met our mother. After they became refugees of a brutal civil war, our parents laid down their roots in Montana, the state that provided our family with the opportunity for a better life. Little did Dad know that 25 years later, he would be leading the community we're proud to call home toward an even brighter future as the Mayor of Helena.
Our dad always inspired us with his dedication to service, from fostering the next generation as a teacher to serving our country in the National Guard, Army reserves, and Navy reserves. We are so thrilled that he's running for Senate because we know that Montanans need more public servants like our dad on their side.
**[To celebrate Father's Day this year, we want to help give our dad a strong start to his campaign. Please contribute $5 now to help elect him as Montana's next Senator.](https://act.wilmotcollins. "https://secure.actblue.com/donate/wc-email?refcode=em190616&email=dpwillis67@gmail.com&zip=18326&firstname=&lastname=&addr1=&addr2=&city=Cresco&state=PA&phone=&employer=&occupation=")**
[****](https://act.wilmotcollins. "https://secure.actblue.com/donate/wc-email?refcode=em190616&email=dpwillis67@gmail.com&zip=18326&firstname=&lastname=&addr1=&addr2=&city=Cresco&state=PA&phone=&employer=&occupation=")
Our dad's example has led us to give back in our lives and careers--Jaymie is serving in the Navy overseas and Bliss is working to elect progressive candidates around the country--and we are so proud that he is taking the role of public servant to the next level: Washington, D.C.

clean_body: Team, This Father's Day, we want to take a moment to tell you a little bit about our dad, Wilmot Collins. Dad's journey began in Liberia, where he met our mother. After they became refugees of a brutal civil war, our parents laid down their roots in Montana, the state that provided our family with the opportunity for a better life.
